solutions.title: Check container logs for the crash cause
solutions.command_template:
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> --previous
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: CrashLoopBackOff means the container keeps crashing on startup. The `--previous` flag shows logs from the last crashed instance. Look for the exit code and last error lines.

solutions.title: Check for missing environment variables or secrets
solutions.command_template:
kubectl get secret <secret-name> -n <namespace> -o yaml
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace> | grep -A5 Environment
solutions.explanation: A common crash cause is a missing required environment variable or secret. Verify all `env` and `envFrom` references exist in the namespace.

solutions.title: Increase liveness probe initialDelaySeconds
solutions.explanation: If the app is slow to start, the liveness probe may kill it before it's ready. Increase `initialDelaySeconds` in the pod spec to give the app more startup time.

patterns.name: Kubernetes OOMKilled — Container Out of Memory

detection.regex: OOMKilled|Reason:\s+OOMKilled|Exit Code:\s+137.*OOM|memory cgroup out of memory

solutions.title: Increase memory limit in pod spec
solutions.command_template: kubectl set resources deployment/<name> -n <namespace> --limits=memory=1Gi --requests=memory=512Mi
solutions.explanation: The container exceeded its memory limit and was killed (exit code 137). Increase `resources.limits.memory` in the Deployment/StatefulSet spec. Start with 2x the current limit.

solutions.title: Check current memory usage
solutions.command_template:
kubectl top pod -n <namespace> --sort-by=memory
kubectl top nodes
solutions.explanation: Check live memory usage to understand how much is actually needed before adjusting limits.

solutions.title: Tune application memory for Node.js / JVM
solutions.command_template:
# Node.js — add to Dockerfile or env:
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=768
# Java — add JVM flags:
JAVA_OPTS=-Xmx768m -Xms256m
solutions.explanation: If the memory limit cannot be raised, tune the runtime. Node.js can be constrained with NODE_OPTIONS; JVM apps with -Xmx flags. Set these to ~80% of the memory limit.

patterns.name: Kubernetes ImagePull Failed — Docker Hub Rate Limit

detection.regex: 429 Too Many Requests|toomanyrequests|You have reached your pull rate limit

solutions.title: Authenticate to Docker Hub in CI
solutions.explanation: Anonymous Docker Hub pulls are limited to 100/6h per IP. Authenticated pulls get 200/6h (free) or unlimited (Pro/Team). Add Docker Hub login before builds.

solutions.title: Use an imagePullSecret in Kubernetes
solutions.command_template:
kubectl create secret docker-registry dockerhub-creds \
  --docker-username=<user> \
  --docker-password=<token> \
  --docker-server=https://index.docker.io/v1/ \
  -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: Create a Docker Hub imagePullSecret and reference it in your pod spec under `spec.imagePullSecrets`. This authenticates Kubernetes node pulls.

solutions.title: Mirror images to a private registry
solutions.explanation: Mirror frequently-used public images (node, python, alpine) to your own ECR/GCR/GHCR registry. Reference your mirror instead of docker.io to eliminate rate limits entirely.

detection.regex: exceeded quota|is forbidden.*exceeded quota|Error from server.*Forbidden.*quota

solutions.title: Check current quota usage
solutions.command_template:
kubectl describe resourcequota -n <namespace>
kubectl get resourcequota -n <namespace> -o yaml
solutions.explanation: Check the current resource quota limits and usage for the namespace. Identify which resource (CPU, memory, pods, PVCs) is exhausted.

solutions.title: Request quota increase from platform team
solutions.explanation: If the quota is legitimately too low for the workload, request an increase from the platform/SRE team. Provide the current and required CPU/memory numbers.

solutions.title: Reduce resource requests in Deployment
solutions.explanation: If the quota is correct, reduce `resources.requests` in the Deployment spec. Requests must be accurate (not over-provisioned). Right-size using `kubectl top pod` data.

solutions.title: Check what permissions the service account has
solutions.command_template: kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --as=system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<sa-name> -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: Use `kubectl auth can-i` to check if the service account can perform the failing action. Run `kubectl describe clusterrolebinding` to see current role assignments.

solutions.title: Create or update the RBAC Role/ClusterRole
solutions.command_template:
# Example ClusterRole grant:
kubectl create clusterrolebinding <name> \
  --clusterrole=<role> \
  --serviceaccount=<namespace>:<sa-name>
solutions.explanation: Create a Role or ClusterRole with the necessary verbs/resources, then bind it to the service account using a RoleBinding or ClusterRoleBinding.

solutions.title: Verify the service account name
solutions.explanation: Confirm the pod is using the correct service account. Check `spec.serviceAccountName` in the pod spec matches the account with the required permissions.

patterns.name: Helm Upgrade Failed — Immutable Field Changed

detection.regex: UPGRADE FAILED: cannot patch .* with kind .*: .* is invalid|field is immutable|spec\.selector.*Invalid value

solutions.title: Delete and reinstall the Helm release
solutions.command_template:
helm uninstall <release-name> -n <namespace>
helm install <release-name> ./chart -n <namespace> -f values.yaml
solutions.explanation: The Deployment's `spec.selector` is immutable — it cannot be changed after creation. You must delete the release and reinstall. Data in PersistentVolumes is NOT deleted (check your chart's `persistentVolumeReclaimPolicy`).

solutions.title: Use helm upgrade --force (deletes pods temporarily)
solutions.command_template: helm upgrade <release-name> ./chart -n <namespace> --force
solutions.explanation: `--force` deletes the resource and recreates it. This causes a brief downtime but avoids manual uninstall. Use only when downtime is acceptable.

solutions.title: Revert the label selector change
solutions.explanation: If the selector change was unintentional, revert the chart change that modified `spec.selector` or `matchLabels`. Selector changes require a delete+recreate regardless.

patterns.name: Helm Upgrade Timed Out — Pods Not Ready

detection.regex: Error: timed out waiting for the condition|Helm.*timed out|timeout.*waiting.*helm|context deadline exceeded.*helm
keywords_required: timed out waiting for the condition

solutions.title: Check pod events and logs after timeout
solutions.command_template:
kubectl get pods -n <namespace> -l app=<release-name>
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace>
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: The Helm upgrade timed out because pods never became Ready. Inspect pod events and logs to find the root cause (image pull failure, OOMKilled, readiness probe failing).

solutions.title: Increase helm upgrade timeout
solutions.command_template: helm upgrade <release-name> ./chart -n <namespace> --timeout 10m
solutions.explanation: Default Helm timeout is 5 minutes. If the app legitimately takes longer to start (e.g. database migrations), increase with `--timeout 10m`.

solutions.title: Roll back to the previous release
solutions.command_template:
helm rollback <release-name> -n <namespace>
# Or to a specific revision:
helm rollback <release-name> <revision-number> -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: Roll back to the last working release to restore service while investigating the root cause.

detection.regex: Error: INSTALLATION FAILED: cannot re-use a name that is still in use|release.*already exists

solutions.title: Use helm upgrade --install (idempotent)
solutions.command_template: helm upgrade --install <release-name> ./chart -n <namespace> -f values.yaml
solutions.explanation: `helm upgrade --install` installs if not present, upgrades if it exists. Always use this in CI pipelines instead of `helm install` to make deployments idempotent.

solutions.title: Uninstall the stuck release first
solutions.command_template:
helm list -n <namespace>
helm uninstall <release-name> -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: If the release is in a failed state and cannot be upgraded, uninstall it first then reinstall.

detection.regex: Health check failed after|timed out waiting for.*condition|Health Status.*Degraded.*timeout

solutions.title: Check ArgoCD app status and pod events
solutions.command_template:
argocd app get <app-name>
argocd app logs <app-name>
kubectl get events -n <namespace> --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -30
solutions.explanation: ArgoCD health check timed out — the Deployment never became healthy. Check pod events and application logs to find the root cause.

solutions.title: Check ArgoCD sync status in UI
solutions.command_template:
argocd app sync <app-name>
argocd app wait <app-name> --health
solutions.explanation: Open the ArgoCD UI to see which resources failed to sync. Use `argocd app sync --force` if resources are stuck in a pending state.

solutions.title: Roll back ArgoCD app to previous revision
solutions.command_template:
argocd app history <app-name>
argocd app rollback <app-name> <revision-id>
solutions.explanation: Roll back the ArgoCD application to the last healthy revision to restore service.

patterns.name: Kubernetes Pod Pending — Insufficient Node Resources

solutions.title: Check node capacity and pending reason
solutions.command_template:
kubectl describe pod <pod-name> -n <namespace> | grep -A20 Events
kubectl describe nodes | grep -A10 'Allocated resources'
solutions.explanation: The pod cannot be scheduled because no node has enough CPU/memory. Check the Events section to see which nodes were tried and why they were rejected.

solutions.title: Scale up the cluster node pool
solutions.explanation: Add more nodes to the cluster or enable cluster autoscaler. For managed Kubernetes (EKS, GKE, AKS), increase the node group min/max size.

solutions.title: Reduce pod resource requests
solutions.explanation: If requests are over-provisioned, reduce `resources.requests.cpu` and `resources.requests.memory` to match actual usage (check `kubectl top pod`).

patterns.name: Kubernetes Pod Failed — Missing ConfigMap or Secret

detection.regex: configmap .* not found|secret .* not found|unable to mount volumes|no secret named|couldn't find key .* in ConfigMap

solutions.title: Create the missing ConfigMap or Secret
solutions.command_template:
# For a ConfigMap:
kubectl create configmap <name> --from-file=config.yaml -n <namespace>
# For a Secret:
kubectl create secret generic <name> --from-literal=key=value -n <namespace>
solutions.explanation: The pod references a ConfigMap or Secret that doesn't exist in the namespace. Create it before deploying the pod.

solutions.title: Use optional: true for non-critical references
solutions.explanation: If the ConfigMap/Secret is optional, add `optional: true` to the `envFrom` or `volumeMount` reference in the pod spec so the pod starts even without it.

detection.regex: Readiness probe failed|probe failed.*readiness|connection refused.*readiness|readiness probe.*HTTP probe failed

solutions.title: Check if the application health endpoint is responding
solutions.command_template:
kubectl exec -it <pod-name> -n <namespace> -- curl -v http://localhost:<port>/healthz
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <namespace> | tail -50
solutions.explanation: The readiness probe is calling an HTTP endpoint that is not responding. Exec into the pod and test the endpoint manually to see what the app returns.

solutions.title: Increase initialDelaySeconds
solutions.explanation: If the application takes time to warm up (loading data, connecting to DB), increase `readinessProbe.initialDelaySeconds` to give it more time before probing starts.

solutions.title: Fix the health check endpoint
solutions.explanation: Ensure the application's health endpoint returns HTTP 200 when healthy. It should not check external dependencies (database, cache) — only application-local state.
